questions.question: The pot is $80 and your opponent bets $20. If you call $20, how big is the pot you can win?

explanations.A: Correct. The pot already holds $80, plus the $20 bet, plus your $20 call: $80 + $20 + $20 = $120.
explanations.B: $100 counts the bet but forgets to add your own $20 call.
explanations.C: $80 is only the pot before the bet, ignoring both the bet and your call.
explanations.D: $20 is just the size of your call, not the whole pot.

questions.question: You must call $10 to win a pot of $40. Expressed as pot odds, you are getting:

questions.question: You are getting 1:1 pot odds on a call. What is the minimum (break-even) equity you need to make the call profitable?

explanations.A: Correct. At 1:1 you risk 1 to win 1, so you must win 1 out of every 2 attempts: 1/2 = 50%.
explanations.B: 100% divides by 1 instead of 2; it forgets to add your own call to the denominator.
explanations.C: 33.3% would correspond to 2:1 odds, not 1:1.
explanations.D: 25% corresponds to longer odds than 1:1.

questions.question: The pot is $100 and your opponent bets $100. What equity do you need to break even on a call?

explanations.A: Correct. You call $100 to win $200, and the final pot is $300. Break-even equity = $100 / $300 = 33.3%.
explanations.B: 50% divides the call by the pot after the bet but before your call — it omits your own call from the denominator.
explanations.C: 100% compares the bet to the original pot only, ignoring that the bet and your call also grow the pot.
explanations.D: 66.7% is the share of the pot you win when you win — not the equity you need to break even.

questions.question: On the flop you have 2 outs with two cards still to come. Using the rule of 4, your approximate equity to improve by the river is:

explanations.A: 4% applies the rule of 2 (one card to come) instead of the rule of 4 for two cards.
explanations.B: 6% multiplies by 3, which is not the standard outs shortcut.
explanations.C: 2% uses the raw out count, far too low for two cards to come.
explanations.D: Correct. The rule of 4 estimates flop equity as outs × 4: 2 × 4 = 8%.

questions.question: The pot is $100 and your opponent bets $50. With 40% equity, what is the EV of calling $50?

explanations.A: Correct. EV = (equity × pot won) - (lose chance × call) = 40% × $150 - 60% × $50 = $30.
explanations.B: $60 counts the winnings but forgets to subtract the times you call and lose.
explanations.C: $10 forgets that the $50 bet is added to what you win when you call and win.
explanations.D: $70 swaps your win and lose probabilities.

questions.question: The pot is $100 and your opponent bets $50. What is the minimum defense frequency (MDF) — the share of your range you must continue with to stop pure bluffs from auto-profiting?

explanations.A: Correct. MDF = pot / (pot + bet) = $100 / $150 = 66.7%.
explanations.B: 33.3% is the bluffer's break-even (alpha = bet/(pot+bet)) — the fraction you may fold, not defend.
explanations.C: 50% compares the bet to the pot rather than using pot/(pot+bet).
explanations.D: That figure mixes up the pot growth and is not the MDF.

questions.question: River: pot is $200 and villain shoves $100. Their range is 70% value (beats you) and 30% bluffs (you beat). Should you call?

explanations.A: Folding is right only when the bluff share is below the 25% break-even.
explanations.B: Indifference requires the bluff share to equal the 25% break-even.
explanations.C: Correct. You beat the 30% bluffs, which exceeds the 25% break-even equity, so the call is +EV.
explanations.D: Range composition matters; you cannot ignore how often villain has value.

explanations.B: Indifference requires the bluff share to equal the 25% break-even.
explanations.C: Range composition matters; you cannot ignore how often villain has value.
explanations.D: Correct. You beat only the 20% bluffs, below the 25% break-even equity, so the call is -EV.

questions.question: Implied odds refer to:
options: The extra chips you expect to win on later streets if you complete your draw
Only the chips currently in the pot
The rake the house takes from the pot
The chips you lose when you fold

explanations.A: Correct. Implied odds account for future bets you can win beyond the current pot when you hit.
explanations.B: That describes direct pot odds, which ignore future betting.
explanations.C: Rake is a fee, unrelated to implied odds.
explanations.D: Folding wins or loses nothing future; this is not implied odds.

questions.question: Implied odds are largest when:
options: Stacks are deep and an opponent is likely to pay you off when you hit
Your draw is already the nuts
Your opponent always folds when you hit

explanations.A: Shallow stacks limit how much you can win later, shrinking implied odds.
explanations.B: Correct. Deep stacks and a paying opponent maximize the future chips you can win.
explanations.C: A made nut hand needs no draw and gains nothing from implied odds.
explanations.D: If they fold when you hit, you collect no future chips.

questions.question: A flush draw that is slightly short on direct pot odds can still be a profitable call when:
options: The board is guaranteed to pair
Implied odds from future streets cover the shortfall

explanations.A: Profitability still depends on the math, just including future bets.
explanations.B: A paired board can hurt a flush via full houses, not help.
explanations.C: Correct. Expected future winnings can bridge a small direct-odds deficit.
explanations.D: With no equity the call cannot be profitable.

questions.question: Implied odds tend to be poor against:
options: A loose opponent who pays off everything
A pot with many future streets left
An observant opponent who shuts down when obvious draws complete

explanations.A: A calling station boosts implied odds, not hurts them.
explanations.B: Deep stacks generally improve implied odds.
explanations.C: More future streets usually add to implied odds.
explanations.D: Correct. If they stop paying when your draw hits, you win little extra, killing implied odds.

questions.question: Compared with direct pot odds, implied odds:
options: Can justify some calls that direct pot odds alone would not
Are always equal to direct pot odds
Make every draw an automatic call

explanations.A: Correct. Future winnings add reward beyond the current pot, loosening the calling threshold.
explanations.B: They differ precisely because they include future bets.
explanations.C: They adjust the threshold but do not justify every draw.
explanations.D: On the river there are no future streets, so implied odds are minimal.

questions.question: Effective stack size matters for implied odds because:
options: It changes the rules of the game
It caps how many chips you can possibly win after hitting
It determines the rank of your hand
Deeper stacks always reduce implied odds

explanations.A: Stack size does not change the rules, only the payoffs.
explanations.B: Correct. You can only win up to the smaller stack, which bounds your implied odds.
explanations.C: Hand rankings are fixed regardless of stack size.
explanations.D: Deeper stacks generally increase, not reduce, implied odds.

questions.question: Reverse implied odds describe the risk that:
options: The pot is too small to matter
Even when you complete your hand, you may lose a large pot to a stronger hand
You always win when you hit

explanations.A: That is just missing the draw, not reverse implied odds.
explanations.B: A small pot limits losses; reverse implied odds are about large future losses.
explanations.C: Correct. Reverse implied odds are the extra chips you can lose when hitting a second-best hand.
explanations.D: That is the opposite of reverse implied odds.

questions.question: Which draw suffers most from reverse implied odds?
options: A set on a dry board
A low (non-nut) flush draw against an opponent who may hold the nut flush draw

explanations.A: The nut flush is the best possible flush and has minimal reverse implied odds.
explanations.B: A set on a dry board is usually the best hand with little reverse risk.
explanations.C: Top set is near the top of the range and rarely dominated.
explanations.D: Correct. Completing a non-nut flush can still lose a big pot to a higher flush.

questions.question: On a paired board, a flush is more dangerous because:
options: An opponent may have a full house or quads that beats the flush
Flushes cannot be made on paired boards
The flush automatically becomes the nuts

explanations.A: Correct. Paired boards enable full houses, creating reverse implied odds for flushes.
explanations.B: Flushes can still be made; pairing the board does not prevent that.
explanations.C: On a paired board the flush is often not the nuts.
explanations.D: Pot odds always exist; the issue is hidden downside risk.

questions.question: Dominated drawing hands (like a weak ace for a non-nut straight or flush) are dangerous because:
options: When you hit, a stronger version of the same hand may stack you
They have more outs than nut draws

explanations.A: Dominated hands by definition can be beaten by a better version.
explanations.B: Correct. Dominated draws realize value poorly and can lose maximally when they connect.
explanations.C: Out counts are similar; the difference is the quality when you hit.
explanations.D: They can lose large pots precisely because they look strong.

questions.question: Reverse implied odds should make you:
options: Discount the value of marginal draws that can be second-best when they hit

explanations.A: Reverse implied odds argue for tighter, not wider, calling.
explanations.B: Their range determines how often you are dominated.
explanations.C: Correct. Anticipated future losses reduce the effective price you can pay for such draws.
explanations.D: Nut and non-nut draws differ sharply once reverse implied odds are considered.

questions.question: About how often does a pocket pair flop a set (three of a kind)?

explanations.A: 50% is far too high; sets are relatively rare on the flop.
explanations.B: 2% is too low — that is closer to flopping quads.
explanations.C: 35% is closer to a flush draw's equity by the river, not a flopped set.
explanations.D: Correct. A pocket pair flops a set roughly 11.8% of the time, near 1 in 8.5.

questions.question: Set mining with a small pocket pair relies mainly on:
options: Implied odds — flopping a set rarely but winning a big pot when you do

explanations.A: Correct. The direct price is poor; profit comes from stacking opponents when you hit a set.
explanations.B: Direct preflop pot odds rarely justify set mining by themselves.
explanations.C: A small pair is usually well under 50% versus a raising range.
explanations.D: Shallow stacks kill the implied odds set mining depends on.

questions.question: A common rule of thumb for set mining is that you should be able to win at least:
options: Exactly your call back, no more
Roughly 10-15x your preflop call when you flop a set
Nothing extra — it is profitable regardless

explanations.A: Winning only your call back does not cover the times you miss.
explanations.B: Correct. Because you flop a set ~1 in 8.5, you need a large payoff to profit, often cited as 10-15x the call.
explanations.C: 2x is far short of compensating for missing ~7 of 8 flops.
explanations.D: Without a sizable payoff, set mining loses money long term.

questions.question: Set mining is least attractive when:
options: Opponents are loose and pay off big
Stacks are shallow so you cannot win much when you flop a set
You are getting a cheap price to call

explanations.A: Deep stacks improve set-mining implied odds.
explanations.B: Loose, paying opponents make set mining more profitable.
explanations.C: Correct. Shallow effective stacks remove the implied odds that make set mining profitable.
explanations.D: A cheap price helps set mining, not hurts it.

questions.question: After flopping a set, your hand is strong partly because:
options: It is the worst possible hand
Opponents can always see you have it
It is well disguised and can stack overpairs and top pairs

explanations.A: A flopped set is among the strongest flop holdings.
explanations.B: Sets are hard to read, which is exactly why they pay off.
explanations.C: Sets can lose to higher sets, straights, or flushes; nothing is guaranteed.
explanations.D: Correct. Sets are hidden and extract maximum value, the payoff that set mining counts on.

questions.question: You have both a flush draw (9 outs) and an open-ended straight draw (8 outs) on the flop, sharing 2 outs. How many clean outs do you have?

explanations.A: Correct. 9 + 8 = 17, minus the 2 shared straight-flush cards = 15 clean outs.
explanations.B: 17 double-counts the 2 cards that complete both the straight and the flush.
explanations.C: 13 removes too many overlapping outs.
explanations.D: 9 counts only the flush outs and ignores the straight outs.

questions.question: A 15-out combo draw on the flop has roughly what equity by the river (rule of 4)?

explanations.A: ~30% is the one-card (rule of 2) estimate, not two cards to come.
explanations.B: Correct. 15 outs × 4 ≈ 60%, so the combo draw is often a slight favorite.
explanations.C: ~15% uses the raw out count, far too low.
explanations.D: ~75% overshoots; the rule of 4 caps near 60% for 15 outs.

questions.question: Why is a strong combo draw often played aggressively?
options: It has fewer outs than a single draw
It has high equity and can win by hitting or by making opponents fold

explanations.A: A combo draw has more outs, not fewer.
explanations.B: Combo draws improve frequently due to many outs.
explanations.C: Correct. Big draws have both fold equity and high hit equity, making semi-bluffs profitable.
explanations.D: A draw is not yet a made hand.
